coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots stabilized silver nanoparticles-based colorimetric sensor for detection of pesticide residues
coredata.dc:creator: Murugesan, Pramila
Moses, J.A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) wereused as stabilizing agents for the development of AgNPs-based colorimetric sensors for the detection of three different pesticides (herbicide, insecticide, and organophosphate pesticide). C-dots were synthesized from gelatin by a hydrothermal route andphysicochemical properties (morphology, phase structure, chemical composition, optical and structural) were characterized. They were found to exhibitstrong fluorescence and absorption at 410 nm and 315 nm, respectively. The synthesized C-dots could act as a stabilizer to prevent the aggregation of AgNPs. The color of Ag/C-dots changed and absorbance diminished with the addition of pesticides. The detection efficiency of pesticides using the Ag/C-dots probe followed the order:imidacloprid > atrazine > chlorpyrifos. Detection limit and quantification limit were calculated as 9.8783 µM and 32.9287 µM respectively, for imidacloprid. The AgNPs functionalized C-dots sensing platform was used to test imidacloprid in water and vegetable samples;recovery was found to be 96.05% and 95.50%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Pesticide detection
Colorimetric detection
Pesticide residues
Biopolymer-derived carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric detection of pesticide residues in aqueous solutions
Surface structure, size and charge analysis
Performance of AgNPs/C-dots for detection of pesticide residues
Optimization of conditions for determination of imidacloprid
Effect of pH and ionic strength level of medium for determination of imidacloprid
Detection of IMD in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The first author thanks the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Government of India for providing fellowship under the N-PDF scheme (PDF/2021/002639).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD spectra for C-dots and Ag/C-dots; (b) FTIR spectra for C-dots and Ag/C-dots.
(a) XPS spectra of C-dots (survey); deconvoluted XPS spectra of (b) C 1s and (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s.
XPS survey spectra of Ag/C-dots; high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s and (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s and (e) Ag3d.
EDAX results for (a) C-dots, (b) Ag/C-dots;(c) EDXRF results of Ag/C-dots.
HRTEM image of (a), (b) C-dots (inset: SAED pattern), (d), (e) Ag/C-dots (inset: SAED pattern of Ag/C-dots) and particle size distribution (c) C-dots, (f) Ag/C-dots.
Zeta potential of (a) C-dots and (b) Ag/C-dots; Particle size distribution of (a) C-dots and (b) Ag/C-dots.
(a) FL spectra of C-dots, (b) FL spectra of C-dots at different excitation wavelengths; Fluorescence imaging of (c) C-dots, (d) Ag/C-dots.
(a) UV vis spectra; (b) Tauc plot; (c) Extinction coefficient and linear refractive index of C-dots and Ag-C-dots.
(a) The formation of Ag nanoparticle formation at different time intervals at UV-absorbance wavelength of 410 nm; (b) Storage stability of Ag/C-dots at different time intervals.

(a) Detection of pesticide residues in an aqueous solution using Ag/C-dots; (b) Effect of incubation time on the detection of pesticide residues; (c) Effect of Ag/C-dots volume on the detection of imidacloprid.

(a) Effect of imidacloprid concentration on the detection of pesticide residues; (b) Linear plot of imidacloprid residue detection at different concentrations; (c) Effect of pH of sensing solution; (d) Effect of ionic strength for detection of imidacloprid.
Determination of imidacloprid in real samples.
Analytical performances of selected pesticide (IMD) detection methods.

row.entry: Reduced graphene oxide/cyclodextrin modified glassy carbon electrode
Electrochemical reduction (based on recognition element) (chronoamperometry technique)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots stabilized silver nanoparticles-based colorimetric sensor for detection of pesticide residues

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Computational Modeling and Nanoscale Processing Unit, National Institute of Food Technology, Entrepreneurship and Management-Thanjavur, Ministry of Food Processing Industries, Government of India, Thanjavur 613005, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Computational Modeling and Nanoscale Processing Unit

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Computational Modeling and Nanoscale Processing Unit, National Institute of Food Technology, Entrepreneurship and Management-Thanjavur, Ministry of Food Processing Industries, Government of India, Thanjavur 613005, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) wereused as stabilizing agents for the development of AgNPs-based colorimetric sensors for the detection of three different pesticides (herbicide, insecticide, and organophosphate pesticide). C-dots were synthesized from gelatin by a hydrothermal route andphysicochemical properties (morphology, phase structure, chemical composition, optical and structural) were characterized. They were found to exhibitstrong fluorescence and absorption at 410 nm and 315 nm, respectively. The synthesized C-dots could act as a stabilizer to prevent the aggregation of AgNPs. The color of Ag/C-dots changed and absorbance diminished with the addition of pesticides. The detection efficiency of pesticides using the Ag/C-dots probe followed the order:imidacloprid > atrazine > chlorpyrifos. Detection limit and quantification limit were calculated as 9.8783 µM and 32.9287 µM respectively, for imidacloprid. The AgNPs functionalized C-dots sensing platform was used to test imidacloprid in water and vegetable samples;recovery was found to be 96.05% and 95.50%, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant information have been included in the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides can have adverse effects on food, water, and the environment, and therefore on human and animal health. Therefore, there has been a surge in research and policy development to promote scientific practices in the application of pesticides. Further, a range of strategies have evolved, focusing on various means to detect their residues. Residues in aqueous solutions have been detected using colorimetric [1], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [2], electrochemical [3], chromatographic [4], and capillary electrophoresis [5] techniques. Among these, colorimetric-based detection has attracted significant attention owing to its rapid detection capabilities, low cost, simplicity, lesser operating time, specificity, and sensitivity [6–9].

Owing to their improved extinction coefficient and specific optical and electronic properties, metal nanoparticle (NPs)-based colorimetric detection has gained popularity. Besides, reports indicate benefits in terms of reliability, rapidity, and portability [10]. Particularly, noble metals NPs such as silver (Ag) and gold (Au) based colorimetric sensors have been used for the detection of various analytes such as heavy metals, microbial species, and pesticide residues, due to localized surface plasmon resonance effects [11–13]. Further, in comparison with AuNPs, nanostructured Ag displays a high extinction coefficient and is, therefore, a promising candidate in sensing applications. Nonetheless, rapid agglomeration and low stability are key problems in AgNPs-based colorimetric sensors and this explains the need for functionalization of AgNPs with suitable ligands having amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and thiol on their surface [14]. Moreover, the conjugation of ligands with AgNPs can enhance their selectivity and sensitivity toward pesticide residues. Polyvinylpyrrolidone, polyhexamethylene biguanide hydrochloride, sodium dodecyl sulfate, graphene quantum dots, and C-dots have been already utilized as ligand materials for pesticide detection systems [14–18]. In recent years, due to their stability, size, enhanced water solubility, fluorescent properties, biocompatibility, low cost, and tunable optical and electrical characteristics, C-dots have gained prominence for various applications [19,20]. For instance, the possible applications in optoelectronic devices have been demonstrated through simple hydrothermal synthesis of high-purity red-emitting C-dots [21]. In another study, altering the structure of C-dots via the addition of metal ions during the synthesis (microwave heating) was found to enhance the purity, quantum yield (73 %), and optical efficiency of luminescent solar concentrators [22]. Similarly, C-dots obtained by solvothermal and vacuum heating approaches could improve the power conversion and optical efficiency of luminescent solar concentrators [23]. Likewise, the C-dots derived graphene coupled with carbon nanofibers and platinum clusters has shown a higher efficiency in energy-related applications [24].

Studies also explain that C-dots can act as stabilizing and reducing agents for the synthesis and stabilization of metal NPs [25–27]. So far, the C-dot stabilized AgNPs have been utilized as a potent disinfectant agent against microbial species; a fluorescent probe for sensing metal ions and a colorimetric probe for H2O2, glucose, and insecticide detection, were synthesized using different routes (biological, direct heating, microwave, hydrothermal and pyrolysis) and involving various precursors (Lycii Fructus, Malva Sylvestris, banana peel, sucrose, oleic acid and l-asparagine)). Until now, no work has been reported on utilizing biopolymer-derived C-dots as a stabilizing agent for AgNPs for pesticide residue detection applications [18,28–31].

Accordingly, the novelty of this investigation is the fabrication and utilization of biopolymer-derived C-dots for stabilizing the AgNPs and qualitative and quantitative determination of pesticide residues in samples. For this purpose, a hydrothermal approach was adapted for synthesizing C-dots from synthetic biopolymers. Various characterization techniques have been adapted to examine the physicochemical properties of C-dots and Ag/C-dots. Moreover, influencing parameters (reaction time, pH of the medium, and salt concentration) for the detection of pesticide residue have been studied. In addition, the stability of Ag/C-dots was tested. This approach was then used to test real samples (water and vegetables).

Chemicals used in this work such as silver nitrate (AgNO3, MW 168 g/mol, purity ≥ 99%), sodium borohydride (NaHB4, MW 37.83 g/mol, purity ≥ 98%), chlorpyrifos (C9H11Cl3NO3PS, MW 350.59 g/mol, purity 98.5%), imidacloprid (C9H10ClN5O2, MW 255.66 g/mol, purity ≥ 99.7%) and atrazine (C8H14ClN5, MW 215.68 g/mol, purity 99.1%) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, Saint Louis, USA. Gelatin, type A. (C102H151N31O39) was obtained from HiMedia, Maharastra, India. In this study, only analytical-grade compounds were employed.

Pure C-dots were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization. In short, 4 g gelatin powder was homogenously mixed with 40 mL of distilled water and the solution was heated for 4 h at 200°C in a hydrothermal reactor before being allowed to cool. In order to remove contaminants in the sample, it was subsequently filtered with a 0.45 µm nylon filter. Deionized water was used to dialyze the C-dot solution through a dialysis bag for 12 h. The C-dots that had developed were freeze-dried and employed in further investigations.

Silver nitrate (100 µL, 100 mM), and C-dots (20 mL) were mixed and the solution was stirred for 30 min. Then, 100 µL of sodium borohydride was introduced dropwise into the reaction mixture and stirred for 120 min at room temperature. The solution color changed to deep yellow and was stored for further analysis. Furthermore, the stability of synthesized Ag/C-dots was measured for 30 days using UV–visible spectroscopy.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an X’Pert3 Powder diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical; UK) with CuKα radiation at a wavelength of 1.5418 Å. The average crystalline size of the Ag NPs was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation (Eq. (1) [32]
(1)
D =
K λ
β C o s θ
Where D is the average crystalline size, k is the Scherrer constant (0.9 to 1), θ is the Bragg angle and β is the full-width half maximum (FWHM). A high- resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM)(JEOL Japan,JEM-2100 Plus) was used to study the morphological structure of the samples. The sample was prepared for HRTEM examination using the drop-casting technique. On the copper grid, a tiny amount of the sample solution was drop cast and allowed to dry for 30 min at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet Nexus 6700 instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA)), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) ((JEOL Japan,JEM-2100 Plus) and energy dispersive X-ray fluorescence (Epsilon 4, Malvern Panalytical United kingdom 1971) were used to measure the chemical composition of the C-dots and Ag/C-dots. Using a multimode microplate reader (Spectra Max iD3; Molecular Devices; USA), the UV–vis spectra of the samples were measured in the range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescent characteristics of the C-dots were measured by using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan; voltage 350 V, excited slit 2.5 nm, and emission slit 5 nm). The energy band gap, conduction, and valance band potential of the samples were calculated using Eq. (2) to (4) [33,34]
(2)
( α h ν )
2
= k
( h ν -
E g
)
Where, Eg is the energy band gap, hν is the photon energy and α is the linear optical absorption.
(3)
E
CB
= χ -
E C

g
(4)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E g
where ECB is the conduction band potential, EVB is the valance band potential, χ is the absolute electronegativity and Ec is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale.

Eq. (5) to (8) were used to determine the optical properties such as linear refractive index, reflectance, extinction coefficient and linear optical absorption coefficient [35].
(5)
α =
2.303 ∗ A
t
(6)
K =
α λ
4 π
(7)

exp
- α t

Where A is the absorbance, t is the thickness, K is the extinction coefficient, λ is the wavelength of the irradiation source, R is the reflectance and no is the linear refractive index. SDT Q600 V20.9 Build 20 thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) instrument (New Castle, DE, USA) was used to examine the thermal stability of the sample at the heating rate of 20°C/min under nitrogen atmosphere (flow rate: 100 mL/min). Fluorescence imaging was performed in a Nexcope –RTC-7 fluorescence microscope (Radical)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of pesticide residues in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para:
Pesticides (chlorpyrifos, imidacloprid, and atrazine) were selected for the study. Chlorpyrifos, imidacloprid, and atrazine are organophosphate pesticides, insecticides and herbicides, respectively. For the detection, Ag/C-dots and pesticides (100 µM) were mixed. After incubation for 20 min at 37°C, absorbance was recorded (wavelength range of 230–800 nm). The limit of detection and limit of quantifications were calculated using Eq. (9) and (10) [36].
(9)
LOD =
3 σ
S
(10)
LOQ =
10 σ
S
where, S and
σ
the slope of the line and the standard deviation respectively.

The feasibility and applicability of the synthesized Ag/C-dots for imidacloprid detection in real samples such as tap water and vegetables (tomato) were tested. For this purpose, the tap water sample (pH: 6.37 and total dissolved solids: 92 ppm) was filtered and then spiked with pesticide residue. In the case of the tomato sample, they were washed with distilled water and cut into small pieces, and ground; the obtained sample was filtered by adding distilled water (25 mL) and adjusted to the final volume of 100 mL. Finally, the analysis was performed for both samples by following the same procedure given in section 2.5 of this article.

C-dots and Ag/C-dots were synthesized and the physicochemical (phase structure, chemical composition, morphology, surface charge, optical and thermal stabilty) properties were characterized using different analytical techniques such as XRD, FTIR, XPS, EDAX, ED-XRF, SEM, TEM, zeta potential analysis, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy and thermogravimetric analysis.

Fig. 1 (a) represents the phase structure of C-dots. The rising FWHM value (1.808) and declining peak intensity of the XRD spectra specify the amorphous nature of the prepared C-dots. The (002) plane of amorphous carbon or graphite (JCPDS file no: 03-065-6212) is shown by the diffraction peak with a center angle of 20.79° (2θ) and an interlayer spacing value of 0.426 nm in the XRD pattern. The interlayer spacing of C-dots in the (002) plane is notable for being bigger than that of bulk graphite (0.334 nm), indicating the existence of abundant functional groups on its surface [37,38]. Three different diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ values of 44.62°, 65.47° and 77.91° associated with the planes of (200), (220), and (311), respectively, suggesting the occurrence of face-centered cubic (FCC) structure of AgNPs by silver ion reduction by sodium borohydride (JCPDS file no: 65-2871). Due to interactions between C-dots and AgNPs, the peak associated with the plane (002) was seen to be slightly moved from 20.79° to 20.06°. The average crystallite size for AgNPs was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation (Eq. (1) and was found to be 63.96 nm.

The functional groups of C-dots and Ag/C-dots were identified by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1(b)). The hydrophilic group's presence on the C-dots surface is shown by the band at 3219 cm−1 and 1447 cm−1, which relates to the primary amino group (NH) or stretching vibration of the hydroxyl (OH–). The presence of OH– is ascribed to the adsorbed water on the C-dot's surface. The peaks centered at 1629 cm−1, 1393 cm−1, 1324 cm−1 and 1083 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of the CO, CC, CO, and COO– groups respectively. As a consequence, findings indicate the existence of OH–, NH, and COO– groups on the C-dots surface, which account for their capacity to serve as AgNP stabilizers and reducers simultaneously. Moreover, the presence of all functional groups results in effective solubility in water [39]. The OH– ions on the C-dots can donate electrons during the Ag/C-dots formation reaction. Likewise, the existence of the COO– group on the C-dots surface can facilitate adsorption towards the Ag+ on the C-dots surface and inhibit their aggregation [40]. The FTIR anlysis of Ag/C-dots showed the strong peaks at 3259 cm−1, 2191 cm−1, 2089 cm−1, 2044 cm−1, 1984 cm−1, 1975 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1. No absorption band was found at wavenumbers 1393 cm−1, 1324 cm−1, and 1083 cm−1, explaining the disappearance of the carboxyl functional group on the surface, owing to the self-dehydration and reaction with –NH2 group on the C-dots surface [41]. This indicates the formation of Ag/C-dots

A high degree of functionalization can improve the long-term colloidal stability of C-dots [43]. Furthermore, the existence of Ag NPs on the surface of C-dots was confirmed by EDAX analysis (Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. 4(b)). Insets in these figures illustrate the atomic percentage of each element that appeared on both C-dots and Ag/C-dots. ED-XRF results also confirm the appearance of Ag on the surface of C-dots (Fig. 4(c)). Based on these observations, the possible mechanism for the formation of C-dots from gelation was hypothesized. Initially, gelatin gets pyrolyzed into small protein molecules; these are then hydrolyzed into carboxyl and amino group-rich amino acids. Amino acids may incompletely decay and get polymerized before being carbonized to form defect-containing more oxygen and amino hydrophilic groups functionalized C-dots. Owing to the occurrence of this hydrophilic group on its surface, the gelatin-derived C-dots are soluble in polar solvents [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface structure, size and charge analysis
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM examination was used to study the structural morphology of both C-dots and Ag/C-dots (Fig. 5 ). It clearly shows that the C-dots displayed a different shape monodispersed and the selected area diffraction pattern confirmed the amorphous nature of C-dots (Fig. 5(a) and Fig. 5(b)). C-dots are smaller in size than bigger AgNPs. Usually, anisotropic structures appear in the existence of a stabilizing polymer that specially binds to one crystal face and causes one crystal direction to grow more quickly than others (Fig. 5(d) and Fig. 5(e)). In addition, the particle size of each sample was measured from HRTEM images using ImageJ software. The histogram image depicts the size distribution of C-dots and Ag/C-dots (inset Fig. 5 (c) and Fig. 5(f)). The Ag/C-dots have a wide range of size distribution (140–220 nm).

In addition, the size of synthesized NPs was measured by using dynamic light scattering measurements as illustrated in Fig. 6 (c) and Fig. 6(d). Particle sizes of C-dots were found to be in the range of 1.45 nm to 347.6 nm, with an average particle size of 126.1 nm. The variation in particle size C-dots between HRTEM and dynamic light scattering measurements was found due to the hydrophilic materials surrounding the surface of the CDs in the solution [45,46]. The polydispersity index was found to be 0.826, explaining that the gelatin-derived C-dots were moderately dispersed. The mean diameter and polydispersity index of Ag/C-dots were found to be 191.4 nm and 0.284, respectively. The lower polydisperisity index confirmed the Ag/C-dots are highly polydisperse. C-dots have a zeta potential of −2.41 mV, indicating that the surface of the particles contains negatively charged OH– and COO– groups (Fig. 6(a)). Dagar et al. (2019) demonstrated that the negatively charged functional groups make the C-dots highly dispersible in water-based solvents [43]. Moreover, the zeta-potential value of Ag/C-dots was found to be -2.64 mV (Fig. 6(b)). The negative value explained the NP’s stability against aggregation. Moreover, the capping effect of C-dots may be responsible for the negative value of the Ag NPs.

Using FL spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption, the optical properties of the samples were evaluated. The significant FL emission that was seen at 410 nm at a wavelength of excitation of 315 nm, is attributed to the C-dots size, superior surface energy traps, aromatic conjugate structure, and higher surface state. Further to investigate the optical characteristics, FL spectra of C-dots stimulated with different wavelengths of excitation between 325 and 415 nm were obtained. The FL intensity of C-dots was improved and the emission wavelength slightly shifted with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 7 (b)). The developed C-dots have the potential to work as multicolor imaging fluorescent probes [47]. In addition, the optical fluorescence image of gelatin-derived C-dots is illustrated in Fig. 7(c). Notably, The FL intensity of C-dots is quenched after the incorporation of AgNPs on its surface as represented in Fig. 7(d).

The characteristic C-dot absorbance spectra were found at 315 nm (main) and 232 nm (minor), which correspond to the π-n* transition of the aromatic CC group and n-n* transition of the CO, respectively (Fig. 8 (a)). The carboxyl/carbonyl functional groups may have originated from the C-dots surface. While the core sp2 hybridized polymeric domains may have provided the source of the CC.

Using the Tauc plot, the energy bandgap of pure C-dots was determined to be 2.86 eV (Fig. 8(b)). Also, the bandgap positions of C-dots are calculated to be 1.14 eV (conduction band potential) and 4.002 eV (valance band potential). For prepared C-dots, a linear refractive index of 12.19 has been found at 460 nm (Fig. 8(c)). The absorbance spectra for Ag/C-dots were seen at 300 nm (minor) and 420 nm (minor), indicating the formation of AgNPs on the surface of C-dots (Fig. 8(a)). The UV absorption spectrum of Ag/C-dots showed maximum absorption at a wavelength of 420 nm, indicating the plasmon absorbance of AgNPs [48]. The peaks formed between 440 and 460 nm were due to the longitudinal plasmon vibrations [49]. When AgNPs absorb the light, the electrons in the conduction band undergo oscillation [50]. The band gap value of Ag/C-dots was calculated as 1.89 eV (Fig. 8(b)). The conduction and valance band potential of Ag/C-dots are 0.60 eV and 2.49 eV, respectively.

The effect of reaction time on the formation of AgNPs via the reaction between the Ag+ and the reducing agent was measured as shown in Fig. 9 (a). It can be observed that the absorption intensity first declines as the reaction time increases, and after the reaction time of 60 min, there is minimal improvement in absorption intensity. Moreover, the absorption band gets slightly shifted into the visible region increasing the reaction time (Inset: Fig. 9(a)). Notably, the appearance of brown color indicates the formation of AgNPs in the solution (Inset: Fig. 9(a)). Besides, the solution color changed gradually as the reaction time progressed. A comparable absorbance peak intensity values of Ag/C-dots were observed over the period of 30 days, explaining the time-dependent stability of Ag/C-dots (Fig. 9(b)).

The thermal stability of pure Ag/C-dots can be evaluated using the thermogravimetric analysis in a nitrogen atmosphere. A minor weight reduction of 13.69% was observed from 35.5°C to 112.3°C, indicating the removal of surface-absorbed water molecules and disintegration of compounds with weak bonds between the quantum-size elements [51]. Fig. 10 shows the thermogram of Ag/C-dots, where a weight loss of around 92.21% was seen at 950 °C as a result of the elimination of the functional group and the decomposition of the graphitic structure [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of AgNPs/C-dots for detection of pesticide residues
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots and Ag/C-dots were employed for sensing various pesticide residues due to their superior optical characteristics. The absorption spectra were measured for the quantification of the pesticide. No changes in the absorption spectra of C-dots after the mixing of pesticides were noted. This indicates that pure C-dots have a light absorption property but do not have sensitivity toward the detection of pesticides. C-dots have a lot of π electrons that accelerate the electron conveying between pesticides and AgNPs [16]. The absorption intensity was decreased when the pesticide was introduced to Ag/C-dots (Fig. 11 (a)). Moreover, the color of the Ag/C-dots solution was changed from yellow to light red color after the addition of pesticide residues in Ag/C-dots as indicated in Fig. 12 (a) (inset). Except for imidacloprid pesticide residues, the UV–vis absorbance spectra for all of the individual pesticides displayed not much significant changes in the strength of the AgNPs when compared to the control sample (Fig. 11(a)). This result indicates that the Ag/C-dots probe prepared in this study shows better detection efficiency towards imidacloprid than others. This could be possible because of the strong interaction between the NH group in the imidacloprid and AgNPs [53,54].

The C-dots provide the requisite stability to the AgNPs, thus keeping them well–dispersed. Moreover, it could create a specific response to the analyte molecules; a bridging structure can be formed between the analyte and metal nanoparticle, which can result in changes in color owing to the aggregation of the nanoparticle [55]. Earlier, it has been reported that Vander wall, hydrogen, and electrostatic interaction associated with the donor–acceptor-based coordination chemistry can be employed for the aggregation of NPs [56,57]. For instance, Chen et al (2020) demonstrated that acetylthiocholine gets readily decomposed under the catalytic activity of acetylcholine esterase to produce positive-charged thiocholine, which can interact with metal NPs through electrostatic interactions, thus inducing the aggregation and altering the color of the solution [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for determination of imidacloprid
ce:section.ce:para: Various physical factors including reaction time, the volume of solution, the concentration of pesticide residues, pH, and salt concentration can affect detection efficiency. The effect of incubation time for the detection of pesticide residues was tested and represented in Fig. 11(b). It was found that the absorption intensity decreased with increasing the incubation time. The absorption intensity was stable after 20 min, indicating that the Ag/C-dots were completely bound with pesticides. Accordingly, an incubation time of 20 min was optimized.

The influence of the volume of Ag/C-dots (100 µL, 200 µL, 300 µL, 400 µL, and 500 µL) for the detection of imidacloprid was studied (Fig. 11 (c)). The sensing response was better at lower volumes of Ag/C-dots. The graph (Fig. 11(c)) reveals that the volume of Ag/C-dots required for the detection of imidacloprid is 100 µL.

Fig. 12(a) shows the UV–vis spectra for different concentrations of pesticide solution injected with Ag/C-dots after 20 min incubation. It is evident that the pesticide sensor based on Ag/C-dots demonstrated a decrease in the intensity with the addition of imidacloprid from 20 µM to 100 µM. Moreover, plotting the calibration curve yielded a more quantitative interpretation of the sensor response (Fig. 12(b)). The plot shows a linear response with an R2 value of 0.99. The LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 9.8783 µM and 32.9277 µM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and ionic strength level of medium for determination of imidacloprid
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of reaction medium pH variation on the Ag/C-dots-based colorimetric sensing platform has been observed and provided in Fig. 12(c). The pH of the medium was tested with variations from 4.35 to 9.99 using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH for the detection of imidacloprid. Notably, the absorption intensity diminished with an increase in the pH from an acidic to a neutral environment. The intensity of the spectra started to improve if the pH was further increased pH to the alkaline range. This result reveals that the developed sensing platform can be used at various pH levels. Excess OH– or H+ ions cause a dramatic shift of C-dots functional groups and the electronic transition of some defects would be hampered [38].

Various quantities of NaCl were added to the different concentrations of imidacloprid solution. Then Ag/C-dots solution was added to this mixture and was kept at room temperature for 20 min. The absorbance spectra were recorded. Fig. 12(d) depicts the results of the impact of different ionic strength levels (0.2 M, 0.4 M, 0.6 M and 0.8 M of NaCl) on imidacloprid sensing capability of Ag/C-dots nanocomposite solution (at varied concentrations). The ionic strength level was found to affect the sensor’s performance. Systematic increases in absorbance intensity were noted with increasing ionic strength levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of IMD in actual samples

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, the study presented how the combination of AgNPs and C-dots can yield a potent sensing platform for the detection of pesticide residues in food. The combination of these two nanomaterials resulted in the differential binding affinity the for detection of pesticides in food samples. The biopolymer-derived C-dots could exhibit enhanced colloidal stability and environmental stability. The synthesized C-dots were used as a stabilizing agent for the formation of AgNPs. Different characterization techniques confirm the formation of Ag/C-dots hybrid structure. Moreover, label-free C-dots AgNPs exhibit excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward the detection of imidacloprid as compared with other pesticide residues. The described Ag/C-dots nanocomposite could detect the imidacloprid concentration of about 9.8 µM with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.99. Additionally, the sensing ability of Ag/C-dots was tested against both real water and vegetables. The recovery rate in water and tomato is 96.05 % and 95.5 %, respectively. The current approach is more effective for sensing other pesticides if modifying the surface characteristics via doping with other metals and incorporation of some surface functional group.
Pramila Murugesan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. J.A. Moses: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The first author thanks the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Government of India for providing fellowship under the N-PDF scheme (PDF/2021/002639).

Doi: 10.1080/03067319.2020.1715382.
